title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply connected to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe and gives our college its name. Students will explore the pūrākau (traditional story) of Kahungunu's daughter who transformed into the kotukutuku (tree fuchsia) whose flowers feed the tūī along the stream banks, understanding how this story informs our kaitiakitanga responsibilities today.
learningObjectives: Identify and use at least 10 Māori environmental terms (e.g., awa, kaitiaki, taonga, mauri) in context when discussing the local stream
Create a simple pepeha that includes their relationship to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Collaboratively develop and present a basic mihi for the stream using appropriate tikanga
Explain in simple te reo Māori how one traditional practice (e.g., rahui, kaitiakitanga) applies to protecting the stream today

activities.title: He Hīkoi, He Kōrero - Walk and Talk
activities.description: Students take a guided walk along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with kaumātua, learning the pūrākau of the stream and identifying native plants/birds using te reo Māori names (kotukutuku, tūī, kōtare, harakeke)

activities.title: Kuputaka Taiao - Environmental Vocabulary
activities.description: Create personal illustrated glossaries of stream-related vocabulary using flashcards with Māori terms, English translations, and student-drawn images of local flora/fauna

activities.title: Taku Awa, Taku Pepeha - My Stream, My Identity
activities.description: Students develop and practice a pepeha that includes their connection to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, recording themselves speaking it for feedback

activities.title: He Mihi mō te Awa - Acknowledgement for the Stream
activities.description: In small groups, students create and practice a simple mihi to acknowledge the stream as a taonga, focusing on correct pronunciation and basic sentence structures

activities.title: Kaitiaki Ināianei - Guardians Today
activities.description: Students create simple 'kaitiaki cards' in te reo Māori with protective messages for the stream (e.g., 'Kia tūpato - he taonga tō tātou awa' - Be careful - our stream is a treasure) to display around the college

resources: Kuia Ringawera from Ngāti Kahungunu who knows the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Local DOC ranger for stream health monitoring techniques
'He Māpuna te Tamaiti' resource book for supportive te reo Māori activities

tasks: Recorded pepeha including connection to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Illustrated environmental vocabulary glossary with at least 10 terms
Group presentation of mihi for the stream using correct pronunciation
Completed kaitiaki card with protective message in te reo Māori

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communication - Level 3: Use basic te reo Māori vocabulary and sentence patterns to communicate about the immediate environment
Te Reo Māori: Language Knowledge - Level 3: Recognise and understand some common te reo Māori vocabulary and sentence structures
Social Sciences: Identity, Culture, and Organisation - Level 3: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people's customs, traditions, and values
Health and Physical Education: Healthy Communities and Environments - Level 3: Participate in communal events and describe how such events enhance the well-being of the community
